elements.sent-2411-001: Toxicity identification in online multimodal environments remains a challenging task due to the complexity of contextual connections across modalities (e.g., textual and visual).
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we propose a novel framework that integrates Knowledge Distillation (KD) from Large Visual Language Models (LVLMs) and knowledge infusion to enhance the performance of toxicity detection in hateful memes.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach extracts sub-knowledge graphs from ConceptNet, a large-scale commonsense Knowledge Graph (KG) to be infused within a compact VLM framework.
elements.sent-2411-004: The relational context between toxic phrases in captions and memes, as well as visual concepts in memes enhance the model's reasoning capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-005: Experimental results from our study on two hate speech benchmark datasets demonstrate superior performance over the state-of-the-art baselines across AU-ROC, F1, and Recall with improvements of 1.1\%, 7\%, and 35\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-006: Given the contextual complexity of the toxicity detection task, our approach showcases the significance of learning from both explicit (i.e. KG) as well as implicit (i.e. LVLMs) contextual cues incorporated through a hybrid neurosymbolic approach.
elements.sent-2411-007: This is crucial for real-world applications where accurate and scalable recognition of toxic content is critical for creating safer online environments.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=16.25cm]{Figures/archV5.png} \caption{KID-VLM framework: The framework combines KD from a LVLM with knowledge infusion from external KGs such as ConceptNet. The input image and text are processed through the CLIP encoders to generate embeddings, which are fused using different fusion mechanisms. \textcircled{ {1}} Knowledge Extraction from Teacher Model. \textcircled{ {2}} Multimodal Learning Framework. \textcircled{ {3}} Knowledge Extraction from KG. \textcircled{ {4}} Joint Reasoning Space: reasoning using the implicit knowledge from teacher model and explicit knowledge from KG for toxicity prediction. } \label{fig:architecture}
elements.sent-2411-008: The rapid expansion of online platforms has led to an unprecedented increase in harmful and toxic content, posing significant challenges to maintaining safe and inclusive digital environments \cite{alatawi2021survey,kursuncu2019predictive .}
elements.sent-2411-009: According to the PEW Research Center, 41\% of Americans have personally experienced online harassment \cite{Vogels2021 , while a recent study reported a surge in hate speech targeting religion during the COVID-19 pandemic \cite{10.1145/3465336.3475111 .}}
elements.sent-2411-010: Online toxic content, particularly in multimodal forms like memes, is often nuanced and context-dependent, making its detection a complex task \cite{Kiela2020, sheth2022defining .}
elements.sent-2411-011: The contextual nature of toxicity poses a major challenge for detection.
elements.sent-2411-012: Harmless-looking memes can also convey harmful messages through sarcasm, irony, or cultural references, potentially reinforcing stereotypes or negative societal norms \cite{Waseem2018, Gonzalez2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: Accurate toxicity assessment requires understanding the complete context, as implicit evaluations can misinterpret tone or intent, underscoring the importance of using explicit context in toxicity detection tasks \cite{kursuncu2021bad,sheth2022defining .}
elements.sent-2411-014: While the problem of online multimodal toxicity detection has witnessed advancements, several limitations remain that our approach aims to address.
elements.sent-2411-015: Existing approaches, including HateCLIPper \cite{Kumar2022 and PromptHate \cite{Cao2022 , rely solely on training data and pre-trained models without incorporating external knowledge sources, limiting their ability to capture complex contextual cues and nuances of toxicity.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: On the other hand, large multimodal models, such as Flamingo \cite{Alayrac2022 and LENS \cite{Berrios2023 , achieve comparable performance but are computationally expensive and challenging to deploy in resource-constrained environments.}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Existing models often struggle with nuanced or context-dependent toxicity because they rely on pattern matching rather than broader contextual knowledge \cite{pavlopoulos-etal-2022-detection .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Our method addresses these limitations by leveraging an external commonsense KG, ConceptNet \cite{Speer2017 , thereby enhancing contextual comprehension and detecting context-dependent toxic content.}
elements.sent-2411-019: In this study, we propose the following research questions to guide our investigation into improving multimodal toxicity detection: RQ1: How can we design a framework that distills knowledge from LVLMs as well as from Commonsense KG such as a ConceptNet to enhance smaller models’ reasoning and improve their ability to detect toxicity in memes by combining both implicit and explicit contextual cues? RQ2: How does the proposed knowledge-infused and distilled framework perform compared to existing multimodal toxicity detection models on benchmark datasets: Hateful Meme \cite{Kiela2020 and onHarMeme Dataset for generalizability \cite{pramanick2021detecting ? We propose a novel approach that combines KD from LVLMs with knowledge extraction from KGs, integrating implicit and explicit contextual knowledge for more efficient compact VLMs.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Using CLIP \cite{radford2021learning as the backbone, we extract visual and textual features from memes, with one text encoder for captions generated by the LLaVA teacher model \cite{liu2024improved and another for meme text.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: The visual and meme-text embeddings are fused using Align Fusion \cite{kumar2022hate , computing a Feature Interaction Matrix (FIM) to align visual and textual data.}
elements.sent-2411-022: We refine the aligned multimodal representation by distilling knowledge from the teacher model using consistency loss.
elements.sent-2411-023: The student model learns to capture implicit contextual cues by aligning its internal representations with those of the teacher.
elements.sent-2411-024: We further incorporate external knowledge from ConceptNet via graph-based reasoning, constructing a joint graph that combines the meme context and KG entities.
elements.sent-2411-025: The fused multimodal and graph-based representations are then used for the final toxicity prediction.
elements.sent-2411-026: Our findings, evaluated on two benchmark datasets, demonstrate the efficacy of this approach.
elements.sent-2411-027: The Knowledge-Infused Distilled Vision-Language Models (KID-VLM) framework consistently outperforms the baselines as the framework's dual-context learning approach (see Figure \ref{fig:architecture ) enables the model to capture both the latent patterns from LVLMs and the explicit relational knowledge from KGs, resulting in a more comprehensive understanding of multimodal toxic content.}
elements.sent-2411-028: Error analysis and ablation studies further underscore the importance of these components, highlighting the nuanced improvements in accuracy and contextual understanding.
elements.sent-2411-029: Early approaches to toxicity detection primarily focused on textual content, employing traditional machine learning \cite{leo_enhancing_2023,saha_hate_2023 and, later, advanced deep learning techniques were introduced \cite{jonathan_feature_2023,karim_multimodal_2022 as per the nuanced and context-dependent nature of the online toxicity problem.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: Multimodal approaches have emerged in recent years that leverage both textual and visual content.
elements.sent-2411-031: Specifically, HateCLIPper \cite{Kumar2022 and PromptHate \cite{Cao2022 have shown promising results by utilizing pre-trained models, fusion methods, prompt tuning, and large datasets.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Researchers introduced another multimodal framework for detecting toxicity in code-mixed videos through cross-modal synchronization \cite{maity_toxvidlm_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Large multimodal models such as Flamingo \cite{Alayrac2022 and LENS \cite{Berrios2023 have further improved the detection performance while their computational complexity poses challenges for deployment in resource-constrained environments, limiting their practical applicability.}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Knowledge Infusion (KI) is a paradigm that integrates external knowledge sources (e.g., KGs) into machine learning models to enhance the representation and improve reasoning abilities by capturing complex relationships in multimodal content, such as images, text, and videos.
elements.sent-2411-035: Incorporating structured knowledge provides models with access to the explicit context that is often missing from the raw training data \cite{zhang2022greaselm,kursuncu2019knowledge .}
elements.sent-2411-036: In health informatics, \citet{sharma2019incorporating integrated domain-specific knowledge to enhance the performance of predictive models for health-related tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Similarly, \citet{mitra2019towards and \citet{manas2021knowledge illustrated the utility of external knowledge in dialogue systems, showing improved contextual understanding.}}
elements.sent-2411-038: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a widely used technique where a smaller student model mimics a larger teacher model to improve efficiency while retaining performance.
elements.sent-2411-039: In multimodal learning, \citet{wang2020multimodal demonstrated KD’s effectiveness in handling incomplete modalities by distilling knowledge from multimodal models.}
elements.sent-2411-040: KD has also been applied in graph-based tasks, such as \citet{hong_research_2023 , which improved GNN performance by capturing community structures.}
elements.sent-2411-041: In contrast to prior work, our approach combines KD with KI by leveraging KGs, as recent past work on online multimodal toxicity detection is limited by their reliance on training data without incorporating external knowledge sources, potentially constraining their ability to capture complex, context-dependent toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our approach, KID-VLM as illustrated in Figure~\ref{fig:architecture , starts by generating multimodal feature representations, which are learned from a large pre-trained teacher model (e.g., LLaVA).}
elements.sent-2411-043: These representations are then enriched by integrating knowledge from ConceptNet, resulting in a knowledge-enhanced representation.
elements.sent-2411-044: The subsequent sections outline the key processes of KD and joint reasoning in greater details.
elements.subsection-2411-003: Multimodal Representation: Aligned \& Distilled from LVLM
elements.sent-2411-045: We use CLIP as the backbone to extract visual and textual features from meme data.
elements.sent-2411-046: The vision encoder processes meme images, while two separate text encoders handle captions and OCR text.
elements.sent-2411-047: The Caption Text Encoder processes captions generated by the teacher model (LLaVA), with its weights frozen during training.
elements.sent-2411-048: For each image-text pair \( D = \{I_i, T_i\} \), captions \( C_i \) are generated using the LLaVA model to capture the implicit meme context (prompts are provided in Appendix Table \ref{tab:llava_prompts ).}
elements.sent-2411-049: The Caption Text Encoder, Meme Text Encoder, and Vision Encoder generate embeddings for \( C_i \), \( T_i \), and \( I_i \).
elements.sent-2411-050: Embeddings \( T_i \), and \( I_i \) are then fused using the Align Fusion method \cite{kumar2022hate , which computes a Feature Interaction Matrix (FIM), modeling interactions between image and text feature spaces: \begin{equation}R = \mathbf{p}_i \otimes \mathbf{p}_t\end{equation} Diagonal elements of \( R \) represent the alignment between image and text embeddings: \begin{equation}\mathbf{p}_i^\top \mathbf{p}_t = \sum_{j=1}^{n} R_{jj}\end{equation} This aligned representation, \( \mathbf{w _i \), is refined through KD, using a consistency loss defined as the Euclidean distance between the aligned embedding \( \mathbf{w _i \) and the embedding from the teacher model \( \mathbf{w _i^{clip\_llava \): \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{KD} = \| \mathbf{w}_i - \mathbf{w}_i^{clip\_llava} \|_2^2\end{equation} This aligns the internal feature representations between the student and teacher models, enabling the student model to capture rich semantic knowledge and implicit contextual cues from the larger teacher model.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: To enrich the distilled multimodal representation with explicit relational knowledge, we use a joint reasoning framework, which extracts working graphs for each data point using concepts from the meme caption and the LVLM-generated caption \( C_i \), and encodes through graph neural networks.
elements.sent-2411-052: We first extract a subgraph \( G_{\text{sub \) (refer Figure~\ref{fig:architecture ) from ConceptNet based on the concepts from the meme caption and the LVLM caption.}}}
elements.sent-2411-053: The nodes in this subgraph are ranked by relevance scoring \( \rho_v \) to reduce noise and focus on the most relevant KG entities.
elements.sent-2411-054: We create a context node \( z\) encapsulating the overall context of the data point.
elements.sent-2411-055: The score is calculated by passing the concatenated text representations of \( z \) and \( v \) through the Roberta \cite{liu2019roberta model, where the relevance score is determined using the perplexity score: \begin{equation}\rho_v = f_{\text{head}}(f_{\text{enc}}([\text{text}(z); \text{text}(v)]))\end{equation} Additionally, for experimentation, we compute the cosine similarity between the concatenated text representation of \( z \) and KG entities \( v \) using MiniLM \cite{wang-etal-2021-minilmv2 (Sentence-Transformer) to obtain the Relevancy Score \(\rho_v = \text{similarity (z, v)\) .}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: We use these two scores separately for the experiments to assess the relevance, retaining the top $k$ (i.e., 750) most relevant entities for inclusion in the working graph \( G_{\text{W \) (\( G_{\text{working \) in Figure~\ref{fig:architecture ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: After constructing the initial working graph, we add the context node \( z \) to integrate the two sources of knowledge—from the LVLM and KG.
elements.sent-2411-058: This context node is then connected to the entities \( V_{\text{sub \) from the working graph with new relationship \( r' \), forming the final working graph \( G_{\text{W \), to prepare for knowledge representation learning in the next step.}}}}

elements.sent-2411-059: We experiment with two different graph neural network architectures for reasoning over the joint working graph \( G_{\text{W \): the Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN) \cite{schlichtkrull_modeling_2017 and the Graph Attention Network (GAT) \cite{velickovic_graph_2018 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: Each architecture is evaluated independently to explore its effectiveness in integrating multimodal representations and KG relationships.
elements.sent-2411-061: While R-GCN applies relation-specific transformations to process messages, GAT uses attention mechanisms to weigh them.
elements.sent-2411-062: In R-GCN, node representations are updated by aggregating information from neighboring nodes based on relation-specific transformations: \begin{equation}h_v^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{r \in R} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}_r(v)} W_r^{(l)} h_u^{(l)} \right)\end{equation} where \( W_r^{(l) \) is the relation-specific transformation matrix and \( \mathcal{N _r(v) \) are neighbors connected by relation \( r \).}}
elements.sent-2411-063: In GAT, attention coefficients \( \alpha_{vu \) are computed between a node \( v \) and its neighbor \( u \), allowing the model to focus on the most important neighbors: \begin{equation}\alpha_{vu} = \text{ softmax}\left( \text{LeakyReLU}\left( \mathbf{a}^\top [Wh_v \parallel Wh_u] \right) \right)\end{equation} The node representation is updated by aggregating the weighted sum of its neighbors.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Once the node representations \( h_v \) for all nodes \( v \in G_{\text{W \) are updated through graph encoding (e.g., R-GCN, GAT), we learn a pooled representation of the graph to generate a single vector representing the entire working graph.}}
elements.sent-2411-065: We use a pooling operation that summarizes node-level embeddings into a global graph-level representation.
elements.sent-2411-066: For graph pooling, we use Mean Pooling: \begin{equation}h_{\text{graph}} = \frac{1}{|V_{\text{W}}|} \sum_{v \in V_{\text{W}}} h_v\end{equation} These pooling techniques condense the graph's information into a single vector \( h_{\text{graph \), which is later combined with the distilled model's representation in the fusion mechanism.}}
elements.sent-2411-067: The pooled graph representation \( h_{\text{graph \) is fused with the multimodal representation \( h_{\text{distilled \) from the distilled model.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: We explored various fusion techniques (Appendix \ref{sec:fusion-mechanism ,Table \ref{tab:ablation-study-fusion ) to combine these representations.}}
elements.sent-2411-069: Gated Fusion \cite{xiong2024gated emerged as the most effective, defined as: \begin{equation}G = \sigma\left( W_g \left( h_{graph} \parallel h_{distilled} \right) \right) \nonumber F_{multimodal} = G \odot h_{graph} + (1 - G) \odot h_{distilled}\end{equation} where, \( h_{\text{graph \) is the pooled graph representation, \( h_{\text{distilled \) is the multimodal embedding, and \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid activation function.}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}G = \sigma\left( W_g \left( h_{graph} \parallel h_{distilled} \right) \right) \nonumber F_{multimodal} = G \odot h_{graph} + (1 - G) \odot h_{distilled}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-070: The gating mechanism learns to balance the contributions of both representations.
elements.sent-2411-071: Final toxicity prediction is made using the fused representation \( \mathbf{F _{\text{multimodal \) from the graph and multimodal embeddings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: The probability of toxicity is given by: \begin{equation}p_{\text{tox}} = \sigma \left( W_{\text{tox}} \left[ \mathbf{F}_{\text{multimodal}} \right] + b_{\text{tox}} \right)\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{F _{\text{multimodal \) is the fused representation, \( W_{\text{tox \) is the weight matrix, \( b_{\text{tox \) is the bias term, and \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid activation function.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The training process optimizes a joint loss function with two components: Binary Cross-Entropy Loss (BCE) for meme classification and Consistency Loss to enforce consistency between the multimodal fusion and captions from the teacher model.
elements.sent-2411-074: The total loss is a weighted combination of these two losses: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}}\end{equation} which controls the balance between the two losses.
elements.sent-2411-075: The losses are weighted using hyperparameters, and the overall loss is minimized using the AdamW optimizer.

elements.sent-2411-076: We evaluate the performance of KID-VLM on two benchmark datasets primarily used for Hatespeech Detection on the HatefulMemes dataset \cite{Kiela2020 and the HarMeme dataset \cite{pramanick-etal-2021-detecting (Dataset statistics are provided in Appendix \ref{sec:datasets ) For fair comparison, we adopt the evaluation metrics used in prior works \cite{Kumar2022, Cao2022 , including AUC, Recall and Accuracy.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In our experiments with the KID-VLM framework, several key design decisions were made to effectively utilize KGs and fusion mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-078: ConceptNet was employed as an external source of explicit contextual information, and we leveraged multi-hop pathway traversal to expand the model's understanding of relationships between concepts.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specifically, we experimented with Hop 1 and Hop 2 expansions.
elements.sent-2411-080: Additionally, we explored different values of $top_k$ nodes, where \( k \) ranged from 250 to 750, to select the most relevant nodes for inclusion.
elements.sent-2411-081: For fusion of the multimodal representations with the graph-based representation, we evaluated multiple fusion mechanisms, including Gated Fusion, Multiplicative Fusion, Bilinear Fusion, and Hierarchical Attention Fusion (details in Appendix \ref{sec:fusion-mechanism ).}
elements.sent-2411-082: For processing the $G_w$, we employed two types of graph networks: GCN and GAT.
elements.sent-2411-083: We utilize multiple baseline compact VLMs on the two benchmark datasets to compare the performance of our approach.
elements.sent-2411-084: We included two baseline models, HateClipper and PromptHate \cite{kumar2022hate, cao2023prompting , from a well-established benchmark based on the Hate Memes Challenge\footnote{\url{https://paperswithcode.com/sota/meme-classification-on-hateful-memes .}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In addition, we incorporated the Multimodal Bitransformer (MMBT) model \cite{kiela_supervised_2020 and CLIP-based models \cite{radford2021learning , specifically CLIP-Text and CLIP-Image, to enable comprehensive baseline comparisons.}}
elements.sent-2411-086: Further, we created versions of the CLIP model, labeled as CLIP\textsuperscript{* , where only the projection layers are fine-tuned.}
elements.sent-2411-087: We also used the LLava in zero-shot settings for a comparison with an LVLM.
elements.sent-2411-088: We use Optuna for hyperparameter tuning.
elements.sent-2411-089: We sweep through GNN hidden dimensions, number of layers, learning rate, fusion methods, and dropout rates.
elements.sent-2411-090: The model is trained for 30 epochs using the AdamW optimizer with learning rate warm-up and linear decay.
elements.sent-2411-091: A batch size of 4 is used on a RTX 5000 with VRAM size of 32 GBs, and model performance is monitored on the validation set during training, selecting the best model based on validation AUC.
elements.sent-2411-092: The GNN hidden and output dimensions are set within ranges of 2 to 512 and 2 to 1024, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-093: The mapping layer dimension is set between 2 and 2048, the learning rate ranges from 1e-10 to 1e-2, and dropout probabilities are selected within the range of 0.0 to 0.9.
elements.sent-2411-094: Additionally, we tune the number of mapping and pre-output layers between 1 and 5, weight decay between 1e-8 and 1e-1, and the loss alpha between 0 and 1.
elements.sent-2411-095: Results on the HateMeme and HarMeme datasets (see Tables \ref{tab:model-performance-hatememe and \ref{tab:model-performance-harmeme ) show that by integrating external knowledge from ConceptNet and distilling multimodal information through the LLaVA model, KID-VLM framework consistently surpasses baseline models HateCLIPper and standard CLIP architectures across AUC, F1, and Recall with highest improvements of 1.1\%, 7\%, and 35\%, respectively for Hateful Memes dataset.( Dataset details in Appendix \ref{sec:datasets ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: A similar improvement was observed on the HarMeme dataset, with approximately 3\% gains in Accuracy, MA-F1, and Precision.
elements.sent-2411-097: On the HateMeme dataset, KID-VLM, with Hop 1 sub-graphs and Gated Fusion achieved the highest AUC of 86.11 with accuracy of 78.20\%, and F1 score of 78.12, outperforming LLaVA and HateCLIPper.
elements.sent-2411-098: In addition, KID-VLM with Hop 2 achieves the highest recall of 76.14\% while maintaining the highest AUC of 86.11, the accuracy of 76.30\%, highlighting the effectiveness of knowledge infusion and distillation.
elements.sent-2411-099: Relevancy scoring using MiniLM(Sentence Transformer, another approach used for relevancy scoring apart from Roberta) for the creation of working graph $G_w$ from ConceptNet resulted in a marginal decrease in Hop 1 accuracy (73.80\%), but improved precision, recall, and AUC, suggesting enhanced generalization.
elements.sent-2411-100: The combination of distilled multimodal representations and external KGs evidently contributes to this overall performance improvement.
elements.sent-2411-101: On the HarMeme dataset, KID-VLM Hop 2 achieved the best performance with an F1 score of 79.08\% and recall of 81.07\%, surpassing the baseline HateClipper model.
elements.sent-2411-102: By leveraging ConceptNet subgraphs and the KD process, KID-VLM effectively captures the nuances of hateful content, even across varying levels of intensity.
elements.sent-2411-103: Gains in recall, particularly in detecting harmful content, demonstrate the value of external knowledge in meme classification.
elements.sent-2411-104: Recall plays a critical role in toxicity detection, particularly when the model cannot detect actual toxic content as it may have potentially grave consequences.
elements.sent-2411-105: In the Hate Meme dataset, the KID-VLM Hop 2 model achieved a recall score of 76.14\%, 35\% and 10\% higher than HateClipper and MMBT, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-106: This improvement is significant concerning real-world scenarios where false negatives (i.e., failing to detect toxic content) can have adverse impacts on individuals being exposed to potentially toxic content.
elements.sent-2411-107: If such a model were deployed by an online platform, this impact could be amplified towards a bigger audience.
elements.sent-2411-108: In this context, a high recall indicates that the KID-VLM model is more adept in identifying the subtle forms of toxicity that might be missed by other models.
elements.sent-2411-109: This is essential for detecting harmful content in nuanced contexts, such as memes that rely on cultural references, sarcasm, or visual metaphors.
elements.sent-2411-110: By leveraging external knowledge from ConceptNet and the KD from the teacher model, the model benefits from a broader contextual understanding, which enhances its ability to generalize and detect toxicity across different datasets.

elements.sent-2411-119: One of the key advantages of incorporating KGs into our toxicity detection framework is the enhanced interpretability of the model's predictions.
elements.sent-2411-120: By leveraging ConceptNet, the model is able to reason more effectively about the relationships both within and between textual and visual elements present in the memes.
elements.sent-2411-121: The nodes extracted from ConceptNet provide semantic context for understanding both literal and metaphorical meanings in the memes.
elements.sent-2411-122: For example, as illustrated in Figure \ref{fig:llava_desc_conceptnet_graph , the KG helps the model establish connections between key terms such as “drinking water,” “coolant,” and “thirst”.}
elements.sent-2411-123: These concepts are not only linked to their literal meanings but also provide insights into the metaphorical associations present in the meme, such as “overheating” being compared to feeling overwhelmed.
elements.sent-2411-124: By grounding the model's understanding in such knowledge-driven relationships, the reasoning process becomes more transparent.
elements.sent-2411-125: Thus, the inclusion of KGs not only boosts model performance but also significantly enhances the interpretability of the model's predictions.
elements.sent-2411-126: The ability to visualize and understand the reasoning behind toxicity classification is a major step toward building more explainable AI systems for multimodal content analysis (for more details read Appendix \ref{sec: interpret-appendix ).}

elements.sent-2411-132: In summary, the results demonstrate that the KID-VLM architecture, through its combination of KGs and KD, provides superior performance for detecting hate speech in memes compared to baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-133: The use of ConceptNet subgraphs and distilled multimodal representations allows the model to better understand the complex semantics of multimodal content, resulting in improved accuracy, F1, and AUC scores.
elements.sent-2411-134: These findings highlight the critical role of knowledge infusion and advanced fusion mechanisms in enhancing multimodal hate speech detection systems.
elements.sent-2411-135: While KID-VLM demonstrates strong performance on the HateMeme and HarMeme datasets, it has several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-136: The model’s reliance on ConceptNet for external knowledge may limit its effectiveness in cases where the graph lacks relevant or niche information, and its generalizability to other datasets beyond these two has not been extensively tested.
elements.sent-2411-137: Additionally, incorporating graph-based methods increases computational complexity, which may hinder scalability to larger datasets or real-time applications.
elements.sent-2411-138: The quality of KD from larger models could degrade when using smaller student models, and there remains a potential risk of bias from pretrained models and KGs.
elements.sent-2411-139: Future work could address these concerns by exploring more diverse datasets, improving scalability, and investigating bias mitigation strategies.

elements.sent-2411-153: Let \( \mathbf{E _g \in \mathbf{R ^{d_g \) and \( \mathbf{E _m \in \mathbf{R ^{d_m \) denote the graph pooled representation and the distilled multimodal representation, respectively, where \( d_g \) and \( d_m \) represent the dimensions of the graph and multimodal embeddings.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Several fusion mechanisms are evaluated for combining these embeddings from different modalities: Gated Fusion applies a gating mechanism to combine the graph and multimodal embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-155: The fusion is computed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{gated}} = \begin{aligned} \sigma(\mathbf{W}_g [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m]) \odot \mathbf{E}_g + (1 - \sigma(\mathbf{W}_g [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m])) \odot \mathbf{E}_m \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{W _g \) represents learnable weights, \( \sigma(\cdot) \) is the sigmoid activation, and \( \odot \) denotes element-wise multiplication.}
elements.sent-2411-156: Bilinear Pooling Fusion computes the bilinear interaction between the graph and multimodal embeddings: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{bilinear}} = \mathbf{E}_g^T \mathbf{W}_b \mathbf{E}_m\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{W _b \) is a bilinear transformation matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-157: HAN Fusion (Hierarchical Attention Network) applies attention weights at multiple levels to the graph and multimodal embeddings: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{HAN}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \alpha_l (\mathbf{W}_l [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m])\end{equation} where \( \alpha_l \) is the attention weight at level \( l \) and \( \mathbf{W _l \) is the learnable weight matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Finally, Multiplicative Fusion combines the embeddings multiplicatively after non-linear transformation: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{mult}} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_m \mathbf{E}_g) \odot \tanh(\mathbf{W}_m \mathbf{E}_m)\end{equation} where \( \tanh(\cdot) \) is the hyperbolic tangent activation, and \( \mathbf{W _m \) is a weight matrix.}

elements.sent-2411-174: Detecting toxicity in online multimodal environments such as memes remains a challenging task due to the complex contextual connections across modalities (e.g., text and visual), demanding both common sense reasoning and contextual awareness.
elements.sent-2411-175: To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid neurosymbolic framework that unifies (1) distillation of implicit contextual knowledge (e.g., sarcasm, cultural references) from Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) and (2) infusion of explicit relational semantics through sub-graphs from Knowledge Graphs (KGs).
elements.sent-2411-176: Experimental results on two benchmark datasets show the superior performance of our approach, Knowledge-Infused Distilled Vision-Language Model (KID-VLM), over the state-of-the-art baselines across AUC, and F1, with improvements of 0.5\%, and 10.6\%, respectively in HatefulMemes Benchmark across variants.
elements.sent-2411-177: Further, KID-VLM demonstrates better generalizability and achieves best performance across all baselines in HarMeme Dataset with an 6.3\% and 3.2\% in F1 and AUC.
elements.sent-2411-178: Given the contextual complexity of the toxicity detection task, KID-VLM showcases the significance of learning compact models (\textasciitilde 500M parameters) from both explicit (i.e., KG) and implicit (i.e., LVLMs) contextual cues incorporated through a hybrid neurosymbolic approach.
elements.sent-2411-179: KID-VLM provides an accurate and scalable approach to recognize toxic content for safer online environments in real-world applications.
elements.figure-2411-016: \begin{minipage}{0.5\textwidth} \includegraphics[height=8cm, width=8cm]{Figures/motivation.png} \caption{Given a meme, we aim to derive the answer by joint reasoning over the knowledge from LVLM, the KG (green box), and reason over toxicity (red box). } \label{fig:kid_vlm_motivation} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.6\textwidth} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-180: Online platforms have been increasingly prominent in disseminating harmful content, challenging the creation of safe digital spaces \cite{alatawi2021survey,kursuncu2019predictive .}
elements.sent-2411-181: According to the PEW Research Center, 41\% of Americans report experiencing online harassment \cite{Vogels2021 , and recent research highlights a surge in religious hate speech during the COVID-19 pandemic \cite{10.1145/3465336.3475111 .}}
elements.sent-2411-182: Detecting toxic content, especially nuanced and context-dependent multimodal forms, such as memes, remains a complex task \cite{Kiela2020, sheth2022defining .}
elements.sent-2411-183: Memes that appear harmless can convey harmful messages through sarcasm, irony, or cultural references, thus potentially reinforcing negative stereotypes or societal norms \cite{Waseem2018, Gonzalez2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-184: Hence, accurate toxicity assessment requires thorough understanding of context, as implicit evaluations can misinterpret tone or intent \cite{kursuncu2021bad,sheth2022defining .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Although online multimodal toxicity detection has witnessed improvements, current methods, such as PromptHate \cite{Cao2022 and HateCLIPper \cite{Kumar2022 depend only on training data and pre-trained models, which limits their ability to capture complex contextual cues and nuances of toxicity.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: Moreover, while larger models, such as Flamingo \cite{Alayrac2022 and LENS \cite{Berrios2023 , achieve comparable performance but require substantial computational resources to deploy in limited-resource settings.}}
elements.sent-2411-187: These models often struggle to effectively address nuanced or context-dependent toxicity due to their reliance on pattern recognition without incorporating broader contextual knowledge \cite{pavlopoulos-etal-2022-detection .}
elements.sent-2411-188: Our approach, Knowledge-Infused Distilled Vision-Language Models (KID-VLM) addresses these limitations by leveraging ConceptNet \cite{Speer2017 , a common sense knowledge graph (KG) that enhances the comprehension of contextual nuances.}
elements.sent-2411-189: In this study, the following research questions guide our investigation: RQ1: How can we incorporate Knowledge Distillation (KD) from LVLMs and common sense knowledge from KGs into compact models, enhancing their effectiveness in detecting toxicity within memes? RQ2: How does the generalizability of KID-VLM compare to baseline methods in detecting toxicity across datasets, HatefulMemes \cite{Kiela2020 and HarMeme \cite{pramanick2021detecting ? We present a novel approach that enhances compact VLMs by combining KD from LVLMs with knowledge infusion from KGs, enriching them with both implicit and explicit contextual knowledge (see Figure \ref{fig:kid_vlm_motivation ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-190: Utilizing CLIP \cite{radford2021learning as the backbone, we extract visual and textual features from memes, supplemented with captions generated by the LLaVA 1.6-NeXT model \cite{liu2024llavanext for additional contextual features.}}
elements.sent-2411-191: These features are then fused using Align Fusion \cite{kumar2022hate to create a multimodal representation, refined with knowledge distilled from the teacher model's caption representations through consistency loss.}
elements.sent-2411-192: This process enables the student model to learn implicit contextual cues.
elements.sent-2411-193: Using graph-based reasoning, we further incorporate external knowledge from ConceptNet, constructing a joint graph that combines the meme context with KG entities.
elements.sent-2411-194: This knowledge-enhanced multimodal representation is then optimized using cross-entropy loss for final toxicity predictions (see Figure \ref{fig:architecture ).}
elements.sent-2411-195: Our findings demonstrate the effectiveness of the KID-VLM framework by consistently outperforming the baselines.
elements.sent-2411-196: Error analysis and ablation studies further highlight the significance of explicit and implicit context incorporated through KD and infusion, providing improvements of 10.6\% and 0.5\% in F1 and AUC, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-197: Further, our approach showcases better generalizability compared to baselines, as seen in our performance in the HarMeme Dataset, where we beat all baselines with an AUC of 92.98.
elements.sent-2411-198: Early approaches to online toxicity detection primarily focused on textual content, employing traditional machine learning techniques \cite{leo_enhancing_2023,saha_hate_2023 , which evolved to utilizing deep learning to address its nuanced, context-dependent nature \cite{jonathan_feature_2023,karim_multimodal_2022 .}}

elements.sent-2411-200: However, these methods lack mechanisms to integrate explicit, structured reasoning, such as socio-cultural norms, necessary for identifying subtle forms of hate in memes.
elements.sent-2411-201: Large multimodal systems, including Flamingo-80B \cite{Alayrac2022 and LENS \cite{Berrios2023 , demand high computational costs, challenging deployment.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Recent work, such as Pro-Cap \cite{10.1145/3581783.3612498 , has attempted to address this by freezing VLMs and aligning textual-visual features for efficient inference.}

elements.sent-2411-204: Efforts to improve interpretability included \cite{Lin2023a distilled reasoning from LLMs into smaller architectures and a multimodal debate framework \cite{Lin2024 that enhances transparency in meme analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Knowledge Enhanced Learning can be broadly categorized into two complementary approaches: (i) Knowledge Infusion (KI), which integrates structured external knowledge, such as KGs and ontologies, into models, and (ii) Knowledge Distillation (KD), where a large teacher model guides the training of a compact student model, helping to achieve similar performance with lower computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-206: KI enhances the model's representations by incorporating external structured knowledge from KGs and ontologies to capture explicit context across modalities \cite{zhang2022greaselm,kursuncu2019knowledge .}
elements.sent-2411-207: Further, \citet{agrawal2023can investigated how KGs can reduce hallucinations in LLMs, while \citet{padhi2024enhancing enhanced cross-modal contextual congruence for crowdfunding campaigns.}}
elements.sent-2411-208: KD process involves transferring knowledge from a larger teacher model to a smaller, more efficient student model.
elements.sent-2411-209: Similarly, \citet{hong_research_2023 applied KD to Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to enhance capturing community structures.}
elements.sent-2411-210: In contrast, \citet{gholami2024gold proposed an out-of-distribution-guided framework that enhances distilled models through targeted data generation.}
elements.sent-2411-211: Further, \citet{wei2024sentence compared sentence-level and token-level distillation on the optimal conditions for each approach.}
elements.sent-2411-212: In contrast to prior work, our approach unifies KD with KI through a neurosymbolic framework that infuses explicit relational semantics from common sense KGs and distills implicit knowledge from LVLMs.
elements.sent-2411-213: This approach aims to equip compact models learning from both knowledge bases and large pre-trained models to boost multimodal reasoning capabilities.

elements.sent-2411-222: Then, this process generates a knowledge representation for working graphs through GNNs \cite{yasunaga2021qa .}
elements.sent-2411-223: To construct the working graph \(G_{\text{working }\), we first extract a sub-KG \( G_{\text{sub \) from ConceptNet (See Figure~\ref{fig:architecture ) incorporating concepts from the meme's text and the teacher-generated caption.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: We explicitly connect this sub-KG in a joint graph with a new context node \(z\), which represents the meme context and connects to each entity from the meme's text and teacher-generated text.
elements.sent-2411-225: The heuristic retrieval of \(G_{\text{sub }\) from a KG can introduce irrelevant nodes, potentially adding noise and complicating reasoning, especially with large sub-KGs \citep{yasunaga2021qa .}}
elements.sent-2411-226: To address this, we apply two relevance scoring methods to assess the semantic alignment between the context node \(z\) and each candidate node \(v\) in \(G_{\text{sub }\): one using the Roberta model \cite{liu2019roberta with perplexity score and another using MiniLM \cite{wang-etal-2021-minilmv2 with cosine similarity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-227: The nodes within this sub-KG are ranked by relevance scoring to minimize noise and focus on the most relevant KG entities.
elements.sent-2411-228: This scoring involves evaluating the relationship of each node \( v \in V_{\text{sub \), where \(V_{\text{sub \) represents the nodes of \( G_{\text{sub \), with the overall meme context, encapsulated by the context node \( z\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: The relevance score \( \rho_v \) is calculated through the Roberta model \cite{liu2019roberta by passing the concatenated text representations of \( z \) and \( v \).}
elements.sent-2411-230: The relevance score is determined using the perplexity score: \begin{equation}\rho_v = f_{\text{head}}(f_{\text{enc}}([\text{text}(z); \text{text}(v)]))\end{equation} where \( f_{\text{enc \) represents the encoder (Roberta) and \( f_{\text{head \) is the scoring head.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-231: A lower perplexity score indicates higher semantic relevance, helping prioritize nodes that align closely with the meme’s context.
elements.sent-2411-232: For additional validation, we compute the cosine similarity between the concatenated text representation of \( z \) and KG entities \( v \) using MiniLM \cite{wang-etal-2021-minilmv2 (Sentence-Transformer) as the relevance score \(\rho_v = cosine(z, v)\) to help identify the contextually significant nodes.}
elements.sent-2411-233: We separately assess the relevance using scores from both Roberta and MiniLM in our experiments, selecting the top $k = 750$ most relevant entities to include in the working graph \( G_{\text{W \) (referred to as \( G_{\text{working \) in Figure~\ref{fig:architecture ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-234: After constructing \( G_{W \), context node \( z \) is added to bridge knowledge from the teacher model with that from KG.}
elements.sent-2411-235: This node is then linked to the entities \( V_{\text{sub \) in the working graph with a new relationship \( r' \), finalizing \( G_{W \) for subsequent knowledge representation learning.}}}

elements.sent-2411-236: To process and learn the representation of the joint working graph \( G_{W \), we employ the Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN) \cite{schlichtkrull_modeling_2017 , which enhances node representations by applying relation-specific transformations to aggregate information from neighboring nodes through structured interactions: \begin{equation}h_v^{(l+1)} = \sigma \left( \sum_{r \in R} \sum_{u \in \mathcal{N}_r(v)} W_r^{(l)} h_u^{(l)} \right)\end{equation} where \( h_v^{(l+1) \) is the updated representation of node \( v \) at layer \( l+1 \), \( \sigma \) is the activation function, \( W_r^{(l) \) is the relation-specific transformation matrix, \( \mathcal{N _r(v) \) represents the set of neighbors connected to \( v \) by relation \( r \), and \( h_u^{(l) \) is the representation of a neighboring node \( u \) at layer \( l \).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-237: In addition, we learn a pooled representation of the graph to generate a single vector representing the entire working graph using Mean Pooling (Appendix \ref{appendix-graph-pooling ).}
elements.sent-2411-238: The pooled graph representation \( h_{\text{graph \) is then fused with the distilled multimodal representation \( h_{\text{distilled \) through Gated Fusion \cite{xiong2024gated mechanism: \begin{equation}F_{multimodal} = G \odot h_{graph} + (1 - G) \odot h_{distilled}\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{F _{\text{multimodal \) is fused multimodal representation and \( \mathbf{G \) is the gating mechanism given by: \begin{equation}G = \sigma\left( W_g \left( h_{graph} \parallel h_{distilled} \right) \right)\end{equation} \( \mathbf{W _g \) is a learnable weight matrix, and \( \sigma \) is the sigmoid activation function.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-239: The model is trained using a joint loss function: \begin{equation}\mathcal{L}_{\text{total}} = \lambda_1 \mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} + \lambda_2 \mathcal{L}_{\text{KD}}\end{equation} where \( \mathcal{L _{\text{BCE \) is the Binary Cross-Entropy Loss for meme classification, and \( \mathcal{L _{\text{KD \) is the Consistency Loss for distillation, ensuring alignment between the multimodal fusion and captions from the teacher model.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-240: We conducted experiments on two benchmark datasets: HatefulMemes \cite{Kiela2020 and HarMeme \cite{pramanick-etal-2021-detecting .}}
elements.sent-2411-241: Detailed statistics are provided in Appendix \ref{sec:datasets .}
elements.sent-2411-242: For evaluation consistency with prior research \cite{Kumar2022, Cao2022 , we used AUC, F1 score, Precision, Recall, and Accuracy.}
elements.sent-2411-243: The HatefulMemes dataset provided two distinct `Seen' and `Unseen' data splits to assess the generalization ability and robustness of each model.
elements.sent-2411-244: The datasets include text annotations for memes, utilized as the text modality, avoiding any influence from LVLM’s OCR limitations.
elements.sent-2411-245: KI: We employed ConceptNet for explicit contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-246: We leveraged multi-hop traversal to expand the model's contextual understanding, specifically experimenting with Hop 1 and Hop 2 expansions.
elements.sent-2411-247: We also varied the $top_k$ most relevant nodes, where \( k \) ranged from $250$ to $750$.
elements.sent-2411-248: This range was chosen because higher values exceeded resource limitations, while fewer than $250$ nodes compromised the depth of contextual information.
elements.sent-2411-249: Fusion: For integrating multimodal and graph representations, we explored several fusion mechanisms, including Gated Fusion, Multiplicative Fusion, Bilinear Fusion, and Hierarchical Attention Fusion (Appendix \ref{sec:fusion-mechanism ).}
elements.sent-2411-250: To process the $G_w$, we utilized RGCN for all our experiments based on our ablation study results.
elements.sent-2411-251: To evaluate the performance of our KID-VLM approach, we benchmarked against several compact VLMs from prior research, which include HateClipper \cite{kumar2022hate , RGCL \cite{RGCL , Pro-Cap \cite{ProCap , Explain-HM \cite{lin2024towards , Mr. Harm \cite{lin-etal-2023-beneath , and PromptHate \cite{cao-etal-2022-prompting .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: We also incorporated the Multimodal Bitransformers (MMBT) \cite{kiela_supervised_2020 and various implementations of CLIP-based models \cite{radford2021learning to facilitate a broad comparative analysis.}}
elements.sent-2411-253: In addition, we fine-tuned only the projection layers of the CLIP, designated as CLIP\textsuperscript{* , and the LLaVA-NeXT \cite{liu2024llavanext was used in a zero-shot setting to compare with a Large VLM.}}
elements.sent-2411-254: Our main baseline reference for all reported values is RGCL.
elements.sent-2411-255: We utilized Optuna to optimize our model's hyperparameters.
elements.sent-2411-256: Our parameter sweep included GNN hidden dimensions, number of layers, learning rate, fusion methods, and dropout rates.
elements.sent-2411-257: GNN hidden and output dimensions ranged from $2$ to $512$ and $2$ to $1024$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-258: Mapping layer dimensions were set between $2$ and $2048$.
elements.sent-2411-261: We also tuned the number of mapping and pre-output layers ($1$ to $5$), weight decay ($1e-8$ to $1e-1$), and the loss alpha ($0$ to $1$).
elements.sent-2411-262: The model was trained for $30$ epochs using the AdamW optimizer incorporating learning rate warm-up and linear decay.
elements.sent-2411-263: We employed a batch size of $4$ on RTX 5000 and L40 GPUs with $32$ and $40$ GB of VRAM, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-264: Model performance was monitored on the validation set, and the best model was selected based on validation AUC.
elements.sent-2411-265: Our analysis shows that KID-VLM, which integrates external knowledge from ConceptNet and distills information from the LLaVA model, outperforms the competitive baseline models from prior research \cite{RGCL, ProCap, Lin2024, cao-etal-2022-prompting across both the HatefulMemes and HarMeme datasets (see Tables \ref{tab:model-performance-hatememe and \ref{tab:model-performance-harmemedual ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-266: On the HatefulMemes dataset across variants, our approach outperforms by 3.2\% in F1 score for the seen split and 10.6\% for the unseen split.
elements.sent-2411-267: It also improves AUC by 0.5\% on the unseen split.
elements.sent-2411-268: For the HarMeme dataset, it shows improvements of 6.3\% and 3.2\% in F1 and AUC, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-269: The KID-VLM with MiniLM and Hop 2 traversal demonstrate overall higher performance on both datasets.
elements.sent-2411-270: KID-VLM with Hop 1 models also perform competitively, often matching or outperforming other models.
elements.sent-2411-271: We observe that baseline models often exhibit a trade-off between precision and recall, whereas KID-VLM models show a notable balance, reflected in higher F1 and AUC scores.
elements.sent-2411-272: This robust performance across metrics suggests the ability to capture the underlying patterns of toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-273: Further, our models' performance on the unseen split of the HatefulMemes dataset highlights better generalization capabilities, suggesting that the broader contextual understanding afforded by Hop 2 allows the model to generalize beyond the training data.

elements.sent-2411-274: Our framework, utilizing both Hop 1 and Hop 2 traversals, demonstrates consistent robust performance on both seen and unseen splits of the HatefulMemes dataset, outperforming baseline models.
elements.sent-2411-275: Specifically, the KID-VLM with Hop 1 and Gated Fusion achieved an F1 score of $78.63$, an AUC of $86.11$, and an accuracy of $78.70$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Notably, the Hop 2 variant achieved the highest recall at $76.14$ while maintaining robust accuracy and AUC, highlighting its capability to detect contextual nuances of toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-277: The KID-VLM models showcase a well-balanced trade-off between precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-278: The integration of MiniLM for relevancy scoring in the creation of a working graph improved performance, which can be attributed to MiniLM’s ability to refine entity selection, leading to a more semantically coherent working graph, enhancing graph-based reasoning by reducing sparsity, and improving relational inference.
elements.sent-2411-279: While MMBT, CLIP, and LLaVA show varied results, CLIP-based models underperform on the unseen split, particularly in precision and recall.
elements.sent-2411-280: HateClipper and RGCL provide competitive baselines, with RGCL displaying an AUC of $86.72$ on the seen split.
elements.sent-2411-281: On the HarMeme dataset, KID-VLM with Hop 2 achieved the highest overall performance with an F1 score of 84.40 and an AUC of 92.98, which represents the state-of-the-art for this dataset.
elements.sent-2411-282: The Hop 1 model also outperforms the baselines, demonstrating the efficacy of the KID-VLM framework integrating external knowledge for meme classification.
elements.sent-2411-283: The improvement between Hop 2 and Hop 1 indicates the capturing of broader contextual cues enhancing performance.
elements.sent-2411-284: Mr.Harm and HateClipper outperform in precision and recall, respectively, while both fall short in recall and precision, showcasing a trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-285: Pro-Cap and ExplainHM exhibit more balanced results, albeit not matching the overall performance of KID-VLM.
elements.sent-2411-286: RGCL notably underperforms on this dataset, in contrast to its performance on the seen split of the HatefulMeme dataset (see Table \ref{tab:model-performance-hatememe ).}
elements.sent-2411-287: The ablation study, summarized in Table \ref{tab:ablation-study-fusion , illustrates the impact of increasing node count from KG on the model performance in AUC.}
elements.sent-2411-288: With a node count of $250$, the model achieves an AUC of $84.91$, which improves to $85.76$ with $500$ nodes.
elements.sent-2411-289: When the node count is raised to $750$, the model yields the highest AUC of $86.11$.
elements.sent-2411-290: This trend indicates that incorporating more knowledge in the model from ConceptNet enhances its ability to discern and utilize contextual relationships within memes, thereby improving its reasoning capabilities and overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-291: On the other hand, it is crucial to ensure the relevance of the nodes to maintain performance quality and avoid introducing noise.
elements.figure-2411-020: { \begin{tabular}{c | c c || c | c c} \# Nodes AUC \# Fusion AUC 1 250 84.91 1 Gated 86.11 2 500 85.76 2 Multiplicative 80.64 3 750 86.11 3 Bilinear 85.26 - - - 4 Hierarchical Att. 85.39 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation studies on KID-VLM for the HatefulMemes Dataset analyze node count and fusion methods. The left section shows that increasing nodes (250, 500, 750) with Hop-1, RGCN, and Gated Fusion improves performance. The right section evaluates fusion mechanisms with Hop-2, 750 nodes, and CLIP, identifying Gated Fusion as the most effective based on AUC.} \label{tab:ablation-study-fusion}
elements.sent-2411-292: Our ablation study (Table \ref{tab:ablation-study-fusion ) demonstrates the effectiveness of Gated Fusion, balancing multimodal and graph-based features to prevent any modality from biasing the fusion process.}
elements.sent-2411-293: Gated Fusion utilizes a dynamic gating mechanism that adaptively weighs the information from multimodal and graph-based representations using a sigmoid-activated gate.
elements.sent-2411-294: This allows for minimizing noise from external knowledge and preserving contextually relevant information, essential for toxicity detection.
elements.sent-2411-295: Gated fusion outperforms others, such as multiplicative fusion, which may diminish key signals in sparse feature space, and bilinear fusion, which may introduce complexity and increase the risk of overfitting.
elements.sent-2411-296: Hierarchical Attention may risk overlooking critical subtle cues necessary for identifying nuanced toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-297: Further, our experiments with GCN and GAT suggest that GCN, with an AUC of $86.11$, provides better performance through uniform feature aggregation.
elements.sent-2411-298: In contrast, GAT, with an AUC of $85.75$, may be more prone to noise due to its attention mechanism (Refer Appendix \ref{appendix-gat ).}

AUC No Knowledge 76.07 73.00 78.00 56.00 85.14 KI-VLM 78.10 77.63 78.09 77.55 85.35 KD-VLM 73.70 73.12 75.33 73.44 85.53 KID-VLM 78.70 78.63 80.57 74.49 86.11 \end{tabular} } \caption{Ablation study on the impact of KI and KD on HatefulMemes Dataset using Hop-1 (750 nodes) and MiniLM for Relevancy Scoring.} \label{tab:ablation-study-parts}
elements.sent-2411-299: The ablation study shown in Table \ref{tab:ablation-study-parts demonstrates the complementary benefits of KI and KD in enhancing toxicity detection.}
elements.sent-2411-300: KI-VLM leverages external commonsense knowledge from ConceptNet, improving Recall by $38.48\%$, F1 score by $6.34$, and AUC by $0.25\%$ over the baseline VLM with no knowledge, highlighting the value of explicit contextual reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-301: Similarly, KD-VLM enhances Recall by $31.14\%$ and AUC by $0.46\%$, suggesting that distilled knowledge from LVLMs improves implicit contextual understanding.
elements.sent-2411-302: The KID-VLM framework, which combines both KI and KD, achieves the best overall performance with an improvement in AUC by $1.14\%$, F1 score by $7.71\%$, Accuracy by $3.46\%$, showcasing the effectiveness of fusing explicit and implicit knowledge for robust multimodal toxicity detection.
elements.figure-2411-022: \includegraphics[width=0.4\textwidth]{Figures/pca_viz_baseline_kid3.png} \caption{Baseline (without KI/KD) vs. KID-VLM (with KI/KD) t-SNE plots illustrating the reduced 3D representation of the dataset after dimensionality reduction. The colors represent the ground truth labels of the data points. KID-VLM's plot shows a much clearer separation between the labeled data points.} \label{fig:pca_viz_baseline_kid}
elements.sent-2411-303: Enhancing representations by KI leads to a more distinct separation between toxic and non-toxic content as shown in Figure \ref{fig:pca_viz_baseline_kid .}
elements.sent-2411-304: Without knowledge-enhanced representations, the separation between these two classes tends to be ambiguous, and the data points are more scattered in the latent space.
elements.sent-2411-305: In contrast, with knowledge, there is a clearer separation through distinct regions with denser clusters.
elements.sent-2411-306: This improved separation reduces the likelihood of misclassifications, which is often caused by ambiguity and unclear separation, especially in borderline cases where the context is crucial for detecting toxicity in a meme.
elements.sent-2411-307: This clearer separation is likely to stem from both explicit and implicit contextual cues learned during the training.
elements.sent-2411-308: KID-VLM minimizes training overhead by using LVLMs only during inference, avoiding large-scale multimodal model training in prior works \cite{Lin2024, liu2024improved .}
elements.sent-2411-309: By distilling crucial knowledge into a compact model with \textasciitilde 500M parameters, rather than fine-tuning a large model, our approach substantially lowers computational costs.
elements.sent-2411-310: This facilitates efficient deployment in real-world settings where compute resources are limited, making KID-VLM scalable for toxicity detection applications.
elements.sent-2411-311: Leveraging multi-hop traversal helps our model to capture extended contextual cues, improving the overall performance on both the HarMeme and HatefulMemes datasets.
elements.sent-2411-312: It achieves particularly high scores in F1 score and AUC for unseen data, indicating a robust ability to handle new and diverse content.
elements.sent-2411-313: This enhanced generalization likely stems from the broader contextual understanding that Hop 2 provides, which is essential for navigating complex, nuanced content.
elements.sent-2411-314: The results demonstrate that the KID-VLM architecture, through its combination of KGs and KD, provides superior performance for detecting hate speech in memes compared to baseline methods.
elements.sent-2411-315: By integrating explicit relational knowledge with implicit contextual cues, KID-VLM better captures sarcasm, cultural references, and multimodal nuances that often obscure toxicity.
elements.sent-2411-316: The use of ConceptNet subgraphs and distilled multimodal representations allows the model to understand the complex semantics of multimodal content better, resulting in improved accuracy, F1, and AUC scores.
elements.sent-2411-317: By distilling knowledge into a compact model (\textasciitilde 500M parameters), KID-VLM achieves these gains without the computational burden of large-scale models, making it scalable for real-world deployment.
elements.sent-2411-318: While KID-VLM demonstrates strong performance on the HatefulMemes and HarMeme datasets, it has several limitations.
elements.sent-2411-319: Additionally, KID-VLM may inherit hallucination issues from its use of LLaVA components, potentially leading to incorrect or misleading inferences in certain cases.
elements.sent-2411-320: We have presented and discussed a few failure cases in Appendix \ref{sec:failure_cases .}
elements.sent-2411-321: Toxicity detection systems can mislabel content or users, especially when dealing with complex inputs like memes that involve irony or satire.
elements.sent-2411-322: The study uses publicly available anonymized datasets, but the potential misuse of such systems in surveillance or censorship remains a concern.
elements.sent-2411-323: GAT employs an attention mechanism to dynamically weigh neighbor contributions, computing attention coefficients \( \alpha_{vu \) between nodes, focusing on the most important neighbor interactions for node updates: \begin{equation}\alpha_{vu} = \text{ softmax}\left( \text{LeakyReLU}\left( \mathbf{a}^\top [Wh_v \parallel Wh_u] \right) \right)\end{equation} where \( \alpha_{vu \) is the attention coefficient between node \( v \) and neighbor \( u \), \( \mathbf{a \) is a learnable attention vector, \( W \) is the weight matrix applied to node features, \( [Wh_v \parallel Wh_u] \) denotes the concatenation of transformed features of nodes \( v \) and \( u \), and \(\text{LeakyReLU}\) is the non-linear activation function.}}}

elements.sent-2411-327: Let \( \mathbf{E _g \in \mathbb{R ^{d_g \) and \( \mathbf{E _m \in \mathbb{R ^{d_m \) denote the graph pooled representation and the distilled multimodal representation, respectively, where \( d_g \) and \( d_m \) represent the dimensions of the graph and multimodal embeddings.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-328: Several fusion mechanisms are evaluated for combining these embeddings from different modalities:.
elements.sent-2411-329: Gated Fusion applies a gating mechanism to combine the graph and multimodal embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-330: The fusion is computed as: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{gated}} = \begin{aligned} \sigma(\mathbf{W}_g [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m]) \odot \mathbf{E}_g + (1 - \sigma(\mathbf{W}_g [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m])) \odot \mathbf{E}_m \end{aligned}\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{W _g \in \mathbb{R ^{(d_g + d_m) \times d_g \) represents learnable weights, \( \sigma(\cdot) \) is the sigmoid activation function, \( \odot \) denotes element-wise multiplication, and \( \| \) represents concatenation of the embeddings.}}}
elements.sent-2411-331: Bilinear Pooling Fusion computes the bilinear interaction between the graph and multimodal embeddings: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{bilinear}} = \mathbf{E}_g^T \mathbf{W}_b \mathbf{E}_m\end{equation} where \( \mathbf{W _b \in \mathbb{R ^{d_g \times d_m \) is a bilinear transformation matrix that models interactions between the modalities.}}}
elements.sent-2411-332: Hierarchical Attention Network (HAN) Fusion applies attention weights at multiple levels to the graph and multimodal embeddings: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{HAN}} = \sum_{l=1}^{L} \alpha_l (\mathbf{W}_l [\mathbf{E}_g \| \mathbf{E}_m])\end{equation} where \( \alpha_l \) is the attention weight at level \( l \), \( \mathbf{W _l \in \mathbb{R ^{(d_g + d_m) \times d \) is the learnable weight matrix, and \( L \) is the total number of hierarchical levels.}}}
elements.sent-2411-333: Multiplicative Fusion combines the embeddings multiplicatively after non-linear transformation: \begin{equation}\mathbf{F}_{\text{mult}} = \tanh(\mathbf{W}_m \mathbf{E}_g) \odot \tanh(\mathbf{W}_m \mathbf{E}_m)\end{equation} where \( \tanh(\cdot) \) is the hyperbolic tangent activation function, and \( \mathbf{W _m \in \mathbb{R ^{d_g \times d_m \) is a weight matrix that transforms the embeddings before interaction.}}}
elements.sent-2411-334: Our model utilizes caption embeddings for knowledge distillation, which has led to a few misclassifications due to subtle nuances in image-text relationships.
elements.sent-2411-335: Below, we highlight three cases where the model's predictions differed from the ground truth.
elements.subsection-2411-025: Case 1: Misclassification Due to Unexpected Context
elements.sent-2411-336: The image in Figure~\ref{fig:case1 shows a woman with curly hair, wearing a light-colored collared top, and reacting with surprise.}
elements.sent-2411-337: The caption reads: "i hear a funny joke about dish washers." The model incorrectly classified this image as toxic.
elements.sent-2411-338: This misclassification likely occurred because jokes about dishwashers are uncommon, and in some cases, household appliances—especially dishwashers—have been used in derogatory jokes about women.
elements.sent-2411-339: Since the meme presents a reaction of surprise to such a joke, the model may have inferred that the joke carried a sexist connotation, even though there is no direct implication of that in the image or text.
elements.sent-2411-340: This suggests the model was influenced by prior associations rather than the actual content of the meme.
elements.sent-2411-341: On the top, a middle-aged Black man in a dark suit has a serious expression.
elements.sent-2411-342: On the bottom, a noose made of blue rope is shown against a black background.
elements.sent-2411-343: The caption reads: "it's what everyone's been waiting for." The model incorrectly classified this image as non-toxic.
elements.sent-2411-344: The likely reason is that the caption did not explicitly mention the man’s identity (Barack Obama), which prevented the model from associating the image with its full context.
elements.sent-2411-345: Without recognizing the specific political or racial implications of the noose imagery, the model misinterpreted the meme's intent.
elements.sent-2411-346: This highlights a challenge in entity recognition—when crucial details are missing from the caption, the model struggles to infer context accurately.
elements.subsection-2411-026: Case 3: Confusion Due to Facial Features and Expression
elements.sent-2411-347: The meme in Figure~\ref{fig:case3 shows a woman with a headband and red nail polish, pressing her hands against her face with a distressed expression.}
elements.sent-2411-348: The caption reads: "don't think this cream is working...
elements.sent-2411-349: This may have been due to the woman’s facial features—specifically, her relatively thin eyes while being in distress—which led the model to incorrectly associate the image with offensive stereotypes.
elements.sent-2411-350: This suggests that certain facial expressions and features in training data may have been disproportionately linked to harmful content, causing unintended biases in classification.
elements.sent-2411-351: However, the image itself is entirely benign, merely depicting frustration with a skincare product.
